Example: Estimate Hazard Function (hz.*)

Source: vignettes/hz-estimate-hazard-example.qmd 
library(TemporalHazard)
library(hvtiPlotR)

These examples correspond to the hz.* SAS files: univariable hazard function estimation with no covariates. The hazard function shape is fit to time-to-event data to characterize early, late, and constant hazard phases.

hz.death.AVC — Death after AVC repair

SAS source: examples/hz.death.AVC.sas

Purpose: Estimate the parametric hazard function for death after repair of atrioventricular septal defects. No covariates — shape parameters only.

SAS (original) 

%HAZARD(
PROC HAZARD DATA=AVCS NOCOV NOCOR CONDITION=14;
     TIME INT_DEAD;
     EVENT DEAD;
     PARMS MUE=0.2 THALF=0.15 NU=1.4 M=1;
);

R translation (runnable M3 example) 

# Self-contained synthetic cohort (replace with real AVCS data in production).
set.seed(1201)
n <- 220
avcs <- data.frame(
  INT_DEAD = rexp(n, rate = 0.7),
  DEAD = rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = 0.55)
)

fit_hz <- hazard(
  time    = avcs$INT_DEAD,
  status  = avcs$DEAD,
  x       = NULL,
  theta   = c(mu = 0.30, nu = 1.15),
  dist    = "weibull",
  fit     = TRUE,
  control = list(maxit = 300)
)

fit_hz

# Predict survival curve on a follow-up grid.
hz_grid <- data.frame(time = seq(0.05, 4.0, length.out = 40))
hz_grid$survival <- predict(fit_hz, newdata = hz_grid, type = "survival")

# Kaplan-Meier overlay
km_avc <- survival::survfit(survival::Surv(INT_DEAD, DEAD) ~ 1, data = avcs)
km_df <- data.frame(time = km_avc$time, estimate = km_avc$surv * 100)

hz_obj <- hv_hazard(
  curve_data   = transform(hz_grid, survival = survival * 100),
  x_col        = "time",
  estimate_col = "survival",
  empirical    = km_df,
  emp_x_col    = "time",
  emp_estimate_col = "estimate"
)

plot(hz_obj) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "Years after repair",
    y = "Freedom from death (%)",
    title = "Weibull parametric survival — AVC death"
  ) +
  hv_theme()

hz.deadp.KUL — Death after KUL procedures

SAS source: examples/hz.deadp.KUL.sas

Purpose: Estimate hazard function for the KUL (Katholieke Universiteit Leuven) cardiac surgery dataset.

R translation (runnable M3 example) 

set.seed(1202)
n <- 260
kul <- data.frame(
  follow_time = rexp(n, rate = 0.5),
  dead = rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = 0.45)
)

fit_kul <- hazard(
  time    = kul$follow_time,
  status  = kul$dead,
  theta   = c(log_lambda = log(0.18)),
  dist    = "exponential",
  fit     = TRUE,
  control = list(maxit = 300)
)

fit_kul

kul_grid <- data.frame(time = seq(0.05, 5.0, length.out = 50))
kul_grid$cumhaz <- predict(fit_kul, newdata = kul_grid, type = "cumulative_hazard")
kul_grid$survival <- predict(fit_kul, newdata = kul_grid, type = "survival")

# Kaplan-Meier overlay
km_kul <- survival::survfit(
  survival::Surv(follow_time, dead) ~ 1, data = kul
)
km_kul_df <- data.frame(time = km_kul$time, estimate = km_kul$surv * 100)

hz_kul <- hv_hazard(
  curve_data   = transform(kul_grid, survival = survival * 100),
  x_col        = "time",
  estimate_col = "survival",
  empirical    = km_kul_df,
  emp_x_col    = "time",
  emp_estimate_col = "estimate"
)

plot(hz_kul) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "Years of follow-up",
    y = "Freedom from death (%)",
    title = "Exponential parametric survival — KUL procedures"
  ) +
  hv_theme()

hz.te123.OMC / hz.tm123.OMC — Time-to-event with multiple phases (OMC)

SAS sources: examples/hz.te123.OMC.sas, examples/hz.tm123.OMC.sas

Purpose: Three-phase hazard model (early, constant, late) for the OMC (Ontario Multicenter) dataset. te123 uses event-time input; tm123 uses the same model with a time-magnitude parameterization.

R translation (runnable M3 example) 

set.seed(1203)
n <- 280
omc <- data.frame(
  lo = runif(n, min = 0.05, max = 2.0),
  width = runif(n, min = 0.05, max = 1.0)
)
omc$hi <- omc$lo + omc$width
omc$status <- sample(c(1, 0, -1, 2), size = n, replace = TRUE,
                     prob = c(0.40, 0.35, 0.10, 0.15))
omc$time <- ifelse(omc$status %in% c(-1, 2), omc$hi, omc$lo)

fit_omc <- hazard(
  time    = omc$time,
  status  = omc$status,
  time_lower = omc$lo,
  time_upper = omc$hi,
  theta   = c(log_alpha = log(0.20), log_beta = log(1.30)),
  dist    = "loglogistic",
  fit     = TRUE,
  control = list(maxit = 400)
)

fit_omc

On this page

Developed by John Ehrlinger.
